context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on the philosophy q about killing 1 to save 5, and tbh it all kinda blurs together rn. can u just explain the act consequentialist view first?
exchanges.tutor:
Start with the core test: an act consequentialist judges each individual action by its consequences alone. So ask: which available act produces the best overall outcome? 

For your prompt, what would that view care about most in a 1-versus-5 case: the type of act, or the total results? And if saving five means one dies, what would that push the act consequentialist to say in a simple case?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give a brief conceptual foothold without supplying a full answer, then use a targeted Socratic question to move the student toward applying act consequentialism to the assignment’s cases. This follows the rules by keeping the response bite-sized, focused on the assignment, and using guided discovery rather than giving a complete solution.

exchanges.student: idk, probs the total results? so i guess save the 5, but then all the trolley/fuel/blood cases start feeling the same to me.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—“total results” is the key starting point. Now push one step further: if the outcomes are the same across cases, would an act consequentialist treat them differently at all?

So for Fuel, Blood, and the trolley variants, ask yourself: do the consequences differ in any morally relevant way, or is it always basically 1 dies and 5 live? Which case seems different to you, if any?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's first step, then differentiate the cases through a Socratic prompt that directs attention to what act consequentialism ignores versus what other theories might emphasize. I will not supply verdicts for each case; instead I will ask the student to identify whether consequences differ across them, which is the key move needed for the assignment.

exchanges.student: that’s where i get lost tbh, cuz it kinda seems like all of them should be the same if outcomes rule. can u just lay out how act consequentialism handles each one?
exchanges.tutor:
You’re very close, so don’t hand the thinking away now. If act consequentialism looks only at which act has the best outcome, then its treatment of each case will track that same rule.

Pick one case—say a trolley case. What are the two available acts, and which one leads to fewer deaths?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should refuse to lay out the full case-by-case answer because that would give a partial solution. Instead, I’ll keep it bite-sized and use guided discovery: highlight the decision rule, then ask the student to apply it to one case first. This stays on-theme, Socratic, and moves directly toward the assignment.

exchanges.student: uh, do nothing = 5 die, switch = 1 dies, so switch i guess. but ngl i still want the whole set compared side by side.
exchanges.tutor:
Right. Now use that exact template for the others: option A, option B, total harm.

Make a tiny chart yourself: for each case, what act is available, and how many die under each? If the numbers come out the same, what would act consequentialism likely say about all of them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will avoid giving the side-by-side solution and instead help the student generate the comparison structure themselves. This keeps the response concise, Socratic, and assignment-focused while moving them toward organizing their own analysis of the cases.

exchanges.student: ok so maybe it says do whichever leaves fewer dead in every case? that feels too easy tho, like i’m prob missing the point fr.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s the core of the view. The hard part of the assignment is probably not stating it, but testing it.

So ask yourself: do you think all the cases really are morally the same just because the body count is the same? Which case gives you resistance, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's correct core insight, then redirect them to the next analytical layer of the assignment: whether act consequentialism gets the cases right. I will use a Socratic question that distinguishes the theory's verdict from the student's own intuitions, without supplying any case verdicts or wording for their paper.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think pushing someone or using their body feels worse than flipping a switch, even if the math matches. that didn’t really clear it up tho—why wouldn’t act consequentialism care?
exchanges.tutor:
Because act consequentialism treats features like “pushing,” “using,” or “directly causing” as not fundamental by themselves. It asks whether those features change the outcome enough to matter overall.

So here’s the next step: what moral principle are you implicitly appealing to when you say pushing someone feels worse than switching a track?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will explain the theoretical reason at a high level without resolving the assignment for the student, then ask a pointed question that helps them contrast consequentialism with a non-consequentialist principle. This remains bite-sized, Socratic, and moves toward the paper’s evaluative part.

exchanges.student: maybe that there’s a diff between intending harm and just foreseeing it? or like using a person as a means? i'm not sure which one fits.
exchanges.tutor:
Both are promising. Don’t choose by vibe—choose by explanatory power.

Which one better separates the cases from each other: intending vs merely foreseeing harm, or using a person as a means vs not? Test one of those against two specific cases from Lecture 13.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge both candidate principles as promising without selecting one for the student. Then I’ll guide them to test which principle better explains the lecture cases, since the assignment asks for a general account. This uses guided discovery and avoids giving the answer directly.

exchanges.student: i’m still fuzzy tbh—can u just give me a clean 2-3 sentence version i could build from?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m your tutor, not your writer. You need to draft it.

Use this structure in your own words: 1) state the act consequentialist rule, 2) say whether it treats the lecture cases alike or differently, 3) say where you think that view fails, 4) propose the principle you think explains the difference. Which of those 4 parts do you want to draft first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for text they could build from, but I must not write or suggest language for their assignment. I should set a boundary, keep it concise, and use guided discovery by giving a minimal structure they can fill in themselves. This supports learning without providing sentences they can reuse.

exchanges.student: part 3 i guess, cuz that’s the bit i can’t make sound right rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Don’t worry about sounding right yet—get the logic right first.

For part 3, answer these two questions: in which case does act consequentialism seem mistaken to you, and what morally relevant feature does it ignore there? Give me your rough answers, not polished writing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not draft the student’s sentence. Instead, I’ll narrow part 3 into a simple argumentative move: identify one case where the student’s intuition diverges from act consequentialism, then state what the theory overlooks. This is Socratic, bite-sized, and directly advances the assignment without giving wording.

exchanges.student: probably the case where u push/use the person, and it ignores that killing them is more direct or uses them as a tool.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a solid start. Now sharpen it: is the problem “more direct harm,” or specifically “using a person as a means”? Those are not identical.

Which one do you think better explains why some 1-to-save-5 killings seem wrong while others seem permissible?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a plausible objection to act consequentialism. I should affirm the progress, then ask a focused question that helps them sharpen the objection into a general principle for the final part of the assignment. I will stay concise, avoid drafting prose, and keep them moving toward their own written solution.
